International Journal for Multidisciplinary Research (IJFMR) E-ISSN: 2582-2160 ● Website: www.ijfmr.com ● Email: editor@ijfmr.com IJFMR240216904 Volume 6, Issue 2, March-April 2024 1 Emerging Trends in Digital Journalism: A Comprehensive Review Dr. Bhasake Ambadas Assistant Professor, Dept. of Mass Communication, PAH Solapur University, Solapur ABSTRACT Digital journalism has undergone significant transformations in recent years driven by advancements in technology and shifts in audience preferences. This research paper explores the latest trends shaping the landscape of digital journalism examining their impact on news production, distribution and consumption. Drawing upon an extensive review of literature, case studies, and industry reports, this paper identifies and analyzes key trends such as data journalism, immersive storytelling, mobile journalism, audience engagement strategies, and the rise of AI-driven content creation. Furthermore, it examines the challenges and opportunities presented by these trends for journalists, news organizations, and society as a whole. By understanding and adapting to these evolving trends, journalists can effectively navigate the dynamic digital media environment and continue to deliver high-quality, relevant, and engaging news content. Keywords: Digital journalism, Trends, Data journalism, Immersive storytelling, Mobile journalism, Audience engagement, AI-driven content creation. INTRODUCTION In an era defined by digital connectivity and rapid technological advancement the landscape of journalism is undergoing a profound transformation. Traditional news outlets are grappling with the challenges posed by the digital age while simultaneously embracing the opportunities it presents. This transformation is not merely about the migration of content from print to digital platforms but also encompasses a fundamental shift in the way news is produced, distributed and consumed. The purpose of this paper is to undertake a comprehensive review of the emerging trends within digital journalism, shedding light on the innovative practices and technologies shaping the future of the industry. By examining these trends, we aim to provide insights into how journalists and news organizations can adapt their strategies to thrive in the dynamic digital landscape while upholding the principles of ethical and high-quality journalism. The digital revolution has democratized the dissemination of information, empowering individuals to become both consumers and producers of news content. As a result, traditional gatekeeping functions have been challenged, giving rise to new forms of storytelling and audience engagement. In this context, understanding and harnessing emerging trends are imperative for journalists seeking to remain relevant and impactful in an increasingly crowded and competitive media environment. This paper will delve into several key trends shaping the trajectory of digital journalism. Firstly, we will explore the rise of data journalism, which leverages the power of big data and analytics to uncover insights and tell compelling stories. We will then examine the phenomenon of immersive storytelling, facilitated by technologies such as virtual reality (VR) and augmented reality (AR), which offer audiences immersive and interactive news experiences. Additionally, we will discuss audience engagement strategies that foster greater interaction International Journal for Multidisciplinary Research (IJFMR) E-ISSN: 2582-2160 ● Website: www.ijfmr.com ● Email: editor@ijfmr.com IJFMR240216904 Volume 6, Issue 2, March-April 2024 2 and participation, as well as the growing prominence of mobile journalism, enabling reporters to deliver news anytime, anywhere. Furthermore, we will delve into the transformative potential of AI-driven content creation, which is revolutionizing news production processes and personalizing the news consumption experience. However, alongside these opportunities, we will also address the ethical considerations and challenges associated with these emerging trends, including issues related to accuracy, transparency, and privacy. By synthesizing insights from scholarly research, industry reports, and case studies, this paper aims to provide a holistic understanding of the evolving landscape of digital journalism. Ultimately, our goal is to equip journalists, news organizations, and policymakers with the knowledge and tools needed to navigate the complexities of the digital journalism landscape and harness the full potential of emerging trends to inform, engage, and empower audiences in the digital age. Background: The landscape of journalism has experienced a seismic shift with the advent of digital technologies. Traditional newsrooms, once dominated by print publications and broadcast networks, are now adapting to the demands of an interconnected, digitally-driven world. This transformation is not only altering the way news is produced and disseminated but also reshaping the relationship between journalists and audiences. The emergence of the internet as a primary source of news consumption has disrupted traditional business models and distribution channels. News organizations are grappling with the challenges of monetization in an era of free online content, while also confronting the proliferation of misinformation and fake news. In response, journalists are embracing new tools and techniques to engage audiences, verify information, and deliver compelling storytelling experiences. Against this backdrop, several key trends have emerged, shaping the trajectory of digital journalism. One such trend is the rise of data journalism, which harnesses the power of data analytics and visualization to uncover patterns, trends, and insights within complex datasets. Data-driven storytelling not only enhances the depth and accuracy of reporting but also enables journalists to communicate complex information in a visually compelling manner. Another notable trend is the growing prevalence of immersive storytelling technologies, such as virtual reality (VR) and augmented reality (AR). These immersive experiences transport audiences into the heart of the story, allowing them to explore environments and events in unprecedented detail. Immersive storytelling has the potential to revolutionize the way news is consumed, offering a more visceral and engaging experience for audiences. Furthermore, audience engagement strategies have become increasingly central to digital journalism practices. Social media platforms have emerged as key channels for news distribution and audience interaction, enabling journalists to reach new audiences and solicit feedback in real-time. Personalization and customization techniques are also being employed to tailor news content to the preferences and interests of individual readers, fostering greater engagement and loyalty. Additionally, the ubiquity of smartphones and mobile devices has given rise to mobile journalism, enabling reporters to capture and share news stories from anywhere in the world. Mobile journalism not only offers greater flexibility and immediacy but also empowers citizen journalists to contribute to the news cycle in real-time. Moreover, advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) are reshaping news production processes, from automated writing and editing to content recommendation systems. AI-driven content creation has the potential to streamline newsroom workflows and personalize the news consumption experience, but it also raises ethical concerns related to transparency, bias, and accountability. In summary, the digital revolution has ushered in a new era of journalism characterized by innovation, experimentation, and adaptation. By understanding and embracing these emerging trends, journalists and news organizations can navigate the complexities of the digital landscape and deliver impactful, relevant, and engaging news content to audiences worldwide. In an era defined by rapid technological advancement and shifting audience behaviors, it is essential for journalists, news organizations, researchers, and policymakers to understand the dynamics shaping the future of journalism. Identifying Key Trends: The study aims to identify and categorize the most significant trends that are influencing the practice of digital journalism. By systematically analyzing the literature, industry reports, and case studies, the research will highlight the trends that are reshaping news production, distribution, and consumption in the digital age. Understanding Implications: By delving into each trend, the study seeks to understand its implications for journalists, news organizations, and society at large. This includes examining how these trends impact journalistic practices, audience engagement strategies, business models, and the broader media ecosystem. Assessing Challenges and Opportunities: The research will also assess the challenges and opportunities presented by these emerging trends. This includes exploring ethical considerations, technological limitations, economic sustainability, and the need for media literacy and education in the digital era. Providing Insights for Practice and Policy: Ultimately, the study aims to provide actionable insights for practitioners and policymakers in the field of journalism. By understanding and adapting to these emerging trends, journalists and news organizations can enhance their relevance, reach, and impact in an increasingly competitive and complex media landscape. Additionally, the study will identify gaps in the existing literature and suggest areas for future research. By highlighting areas that warrant further investigation, the research aims to contribute to ongoing discussions and debates surrounding the future of digital journalism. In summary, the purpose of this study is to provide a comprehensive overview of the emerging trends in digital journalism, offering insights and recommendations for stakeholders to navigate the evolving media landscape effectively. By understanding these trends and their implications, journalists and news organizations can adapt their practices and strategies to meet the challenges and opportunities of the digital age. Data Journalism: Unveiling Insights through Data: Data journalism represents a significant evolution in the practice of journalism, leveraging the vast amounts of data available in the digital age to uncover stories, trends, and insights that traditional reporting methods might overlook. This section delves into the definition, evolution, impact, and challenges of data journalism in the contemporary media landscape. Definition and Evolution Origins: Data journalism traces its roots back to the early days of computer-assisted reporting in the 20th century, but it has gained prominence with the proliferation of digital technologies and the democratization of data access. Definition: Data journalism involves the collection, analysis, and visualization of data to tell stories and provide insights on complex issues. It encompasses a range of techniques, from simple data analysis to sophisticated statistical modeling and machine learning algorithms. Impact on Journalism Practices Enhanced Storytelling: Data journalism enables journalists to enrich their stories with empirical evidence, providing readers with a deeper understanding of complex issues. Increased Transparency: By grounding their reporting in data, journalists can enhance the transparency and credibility of their work, fostering trust with their audiences. International Journal for Multidisciplinary Research (IJFMR) E-ISSN: 2582-2160 ● Website: www.ijfmr.com ● Email: editor@ijfmr.com IJFMR240216904 Volume 6, Issue 2, March-April 2024 4 Empowerment of Journalists: Data journalism empowers journalists to go beyond traditional sources and narratives, allowing them to uncover stories that might otherwise remain hidden. Case Studies and Examples Investigative Reporting: Data journalism has been instrumental in uncovering corruption, fraud, and other abuses of power. For example, investigative journalists have used data analysis to expose financial irregularities, environmental violations, and government misconduct. Data Visualization: Interactive data visualizations have become a hallmark of data journalism, making complex information accessible and engaging for readers. Examples include interactive maps, charts, and info graphics that allow users to explore data and draw their own conclusions. Challenges and Opportunities Data Quality and Accuracy: Ensuring the accuracy and reliability of data sources is a key challenge for data journalists, who must navigate issues such as data bias, incompleteness, and manipulation. Technical Skills: Data journalism requires a diverse skill set, including proficiency in data analysis, statistics, programming, and data visualization tools. Journalists may need to undergo additional training to acquire these skills. Ethical Considerations: Data journalism raises ethical questions related to privacy, consent, and the responsible use of data. Journalists must adhere to ethical guidelines and best practices to ensure the integrity and legality of their work. Data journalism represents a powerful tool for journalists seeking to uncover stories, provide insights, and engage audiences in the digital age. By harnessing the power of data, journalists can enhance the depth, credibility, and impact of their reporting, ultimately fulfilling the watchdog role of journalism in society. However, data journalism also presents challenges that must be addressed, including issues related to data quality, technical skills, and ethical considerations. Nonetheless, the potential of data journalism to inform, empower, and inspire makes it an indispensable component of modern journalism practice. 1. IMMERSIVE STORYTELLING: ENGAGING AUDIENCES IN NEW DIMENSIONS IMMERSIVE Storytelling represents a paradigm shift in journalism, allowing audiences to become active participants in the news experience by transporting them into the heart of the story. This section explores the concept, technologies, impact, and ethical considerations of immersive storytelling in the context of digital journalism. Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR) Definition: Immersive storytelling utilizes virtual reality (VR) and augmented reality (AR) technologies to create immersive, interactive narratives. Virtual Reality (VR): VR immerses users in a digital environment, allowing them to explore and interact with a simulated world. Augmented Reality (AR): AR overlays digital content onto the real world, enhancing the user's perception of reality by adding digital elements to their surroundings. Interactive Multimedia Features 360-Degree Video: 360-degree video allows users to view a scene from all angles, providing a sense of presence and immersion. International Journal for Multidisciplinary Research (IJFMR) E-ISSN: 2582-2160 ● Website: www.ijfmr.com ● Email: editor@ijfmr.com IJFMR240216904 Volume 6, Issue 2, March-April 2024 5 Interactive Graphics: Interactive graphics enable users to manipulate data visualizations and explore information in a dynamic and engaging manner. Gamification: Gamification techniques, such as branching narratives and decision trees, encourage user participation and enhance the storytelling experience. Enhancing User Experience Engagement: Immersive storytelling captivates audiences by offering a more immersive and interactive experience than traditional media formats. Empathy: Immersive storytelling has the potential to foster empathy by allowing users to experience events and perspectives firsthand. Retention: Studies have shown that immersive storytelling can increase information retention and comprehension compared to traditional media formats. Ethical Considerations Accuracy: Maintaining accuracy and fidelity to the facts is paramount in immersive storytelling, as the line between reality and fiction can blur in virtual environments. Representation: Immersive storytelling raises questions about representation and inclusivity, as creators must ensure that their narratives reflect the diversity of human experiences. Privacy: Immersive storytelling may raise privacy concerns, particularly in the case of location-based AR experiences that collect user data or footage of real-world environments. Case Studies The New York Times' "The Displaced": This VR documentary transports viewers into the lives of three refugee children, providing a visceral and immersive portrayal of their experiences. BBC's "1943 Berlin Blitz": This AR experience allows users to explore a bombed-out street in World War II-era Berlin, providing a compelling glimpse into history. Immersive storytelling represents a groundbreaking approach to journalism that has the potential to revolutionize the way news is consumed and understood. By leveraging VR and AR technologies, journalists can create immersive, interactive narratives that engage audiences on a deeper level and foster empathy and understanding. However, as with any emerging technology, immersive storytelling also presents ethical considerations that must be carefully navigated to ensure responsible and impactful storytelling. Nonetheless, the power of immersive storytelling to captivate, educate, and inspire makes it an exciting frontier for the future of digital journalism. 2. MOBILE JOURNALISM: REPORTING ANYTIME, ANYWHERE Mobile journalism, often referred to as "mojo," has emerged as a powerful tool for journalists to report on events and share stories from virtually anywhere in the world using their smartphones or other mobile devices. This section explores the rise of mobile journalism, its impact on news reporting, the tools and techniques involved, as well as the challenges and opportunities it presents. Rise of Mobile Reporting Ubiquity of Smartphones: The widespread availability of smartphones equipped with high-quality cameras, audio recorders, and editing software has democratized the process of news gathering and dissemination. Real-Time Reporting: Mobile journalism enables reporters to capture and share news stories as they unfold, providing audiences with real-time updates and insights. International Journal for Multidisciplinary Research (IJFMR) E-ISSN: 2582-2160 ● Website: www.ijfmr.com ● Email: editor@ijfmr.com IJFMR240216904 Volume 6, Issue 2, March-April 2024 6 Accessibility: Mobile journalism empowers citizen journalists and freelance reporters to contribute to the news cycle, bypassing traditional gatekeepers and newsroom hierarchies. Tools and Techniques Smartphone Cameras: Advances in smartphone camera technology have made it possible to capture high-quality photos and videos suitable for broadcast and publication. Audio Recording Apps: Mobile journalists often use audio recording apps to conduct interviews and gather soundbites in the field. Editing Software: A variety of editing apps are available for smartphones, allowing journalists to edit and produce multimedia content on the go. Live Streaming Platforms: Live streaming platforms such as Facebook Live, Periscope, and Instagram Live enable journalists to broadcast live coverage of events directly from their mobile devices. Challenges of Mobile Journalism Technical Limitations: Despite advancements in mobile technology, smartphones have inherent limitations in terms of battery life, storage capacity, and processing power. Ethical Considerations: Mobile journalists must navigate ethical considerations such as privacy, consent, and verification while reporting in real-time. Security Risks: Reporting from the field can expose journalists to physical and digital security risks, particularly in conflict zones or volatile environments. Case Studies Arab Spring: During the Arab Spring uprisings, citizen journalists used mobile devices to document and share footage of protests and government crackdowns, circumventing traditional media censorship. Natural Disasters: Mobile journalists have played a crucial role in reporting on natural disasters, providing real-time updates and visual documentation of the impact on affected 3. AUDIENCE ENGAGEMENT STRATEGIES: FROM PASSIVE CONSUMERS TO ACTIVE PARTICIPANTS In the digital age, audience engagement has become a cornerstone of successful journalism, transforming audiences from passive consumers into active participants in the news process. This section explores the strategies used by journalists and news organizations to engage audiences, foster interaction, and build community. Social Media Integration Two-Way Communication: Social media platforms such as Twitter, Facebook, and Instagram allow journalists to interact directly with their audience, soliciting feedback, answering questions, and sharing updates in real-time. Content Distribution: Social media platforms serve as valuable distribution channels, amplifying the reach of news content and driving traffic to news websites and apps. Engagement Metrics: Social media analytics provide insights into audience behavior, preferences, and demographics, informing content strategies and audience engagement efforts. Personalization and Customization Tailored Content: News organizations use data analytics and algorithms to personalize content recommendations based on user preferences, behavior, and demographics. Customizable Experiences: Some news apps and websites allow users to customize their news feeds, selecting topics of interest and setting preferences for content delivery. International Journal for Multidisciplinary Research (IJFMR) E-ISSN: 2582-2160 ● Website: www.ijfmr.com ● Email: editor@ijfmr.com IJFMR240216904 Volume 6, Issue 2, March-April 2024 7 User-generated Content: News organizations may invite audiences to contribute user-generated content, such as photos, videos, and eyewitness accounts, to enhance news coverage and foster a sense of community participation. Community Building Initiatives Online Forums and Communities: News organizations may host online forums, discussion boards, or Facebook groups where audiences can engage in discussions, share opinions, and connect with fellow community members. Events and Meetups: Some news organizations organize events, workshops, or meetups to engage with their audience in person, fostering a sense of belonging and community spirit. Crowdsourcing Projects: News organizations may engage their audience in crowdsourcing projects, such as investigative reporting or data collection initiatives, to tap into the collective knowledge and resources of the community. Metrics and Analytics Audience Engagement Metrics: News organizations track metrics such as page views, time spent on page, social shares, comments, and click-through rates to measure audience engagement and assess the effectiveness of content strategies. A/B Testing: A/B testing allows news organizations to experiment with different content formats, headlines, and engagement strategies to optimize audience engagement and conversion rates. Audience Feedback: Soliciting audience feedback through surveys, polls, and user-generated content submissions provides valuable insights into audience preferences, interests, and needs. Audience engagement strategies play a crucial role in modern journalism, fostering meaningful interactions between journalists and their audience and building a sense of community around news organizations. By embracing social media integration, personalization, community building initiatives, and analytics-driven approaches, journalists can create more engaging, relevant, and impactful journalism that resonates with audiences in the digital age. 4. AI-DRIVEN CONTENT CREATION: THE FUTURE OF NEWS PRODUCTION: Artificial Intelligence (AI) has emerged as a transformative force in news production, offering journalists and news organizations powerful tools to automate content creation, personalize news delivery, and enhance audience engagement. This section explores the applications, implications, challenges, and opportunities of AI-driven content creation in the context of digital journalism. Automated Writing and Editing Natural Language Generation (NLG): AI algorithms can generate written content, such as news articles, summaries, and reports, based on structured data inputs or predefined templates. Automated Fact-Checking: AI-powered fact-checking tools can analyze large volumes of information to identify inaccuracies, biases, and misinformation in news articles. Automated Editing: AI algorithms can assist journalists in editing and proofreading written content, improving grammar, style, and readability. Content Recommendation Systems Personalization: AI algorithms analyze user behavior, preferences, and demographics to deliver personalized news recommendations, increasing user engagement and retention. Dynamic Content Creation: Content recommendation systems can dynamically generate and curate news articles, videos, and multimedia content based on real-time trends, events, and user interests. International Journal for Multidisciplinary Research (IJFMR) E-ISSN: 2582-2160 ● Website: www.ijfmr.com ● Email: editor@ijfmr.com IJFMR240216904 Volume 6, Issue 2, March-April 2024 8 Contextualization: AI-driven content recommendation systems contextualize news content by providing background information, related articles, and multimedia resources to enhance user understanding and engagement. Ethical Implications Transparency: AI-driven content creation raises concerns about transparency and accountability, as users may not be aware that they are interacting with AI-generated content. Bias and Fairness: AI algorithms may inadvertently perpetuate biases present in training data, leading to skewed or misleading news coverage. Quality Control: Maintaining editorial standards and ensuring the accuracy, credibility, and fairness of AI-generated content is essential to uphold journalistic integrity and trust. Human-AI Collaboration Augmented Journalism: AI technologies augment human journalists' capabilities by automating routine tasks, such as data analysis, fact-checking, and content production, allowing journalists to focus on higher-order tasks, such as investigative reporting and storytelling. Editorial Oversight: Human journalists provide editorial oversight and quality control throughout the content creation process, ensuring that AI-generated content meets ethical and professional standards. Training and Development: Journalists may require additional training and support to effectively collaborate with AI technologies and leverage their capabilities to enhance news production and audience engagement. Case Studies and Examples Reuters' AI Journalism: Reuters employs AI-driven tools, such as Lynx Insight and News Tracer, to automate content creation, analyze data trends, and deliver personalized news recommendations to users. The Washington Post's Heliograf: The Washington Post's Heliograf AI system generates automated news updates, such as sports scores and election results, providing real-time updates to readers on digital platforms. In conclusion, AI-driven content creation represents a paradigm shift in news production, offering journalists and news organizations unprecedented opportunities to automate routine tasks, personalize news delivery, and enhance audience engagement. However, AI-driven content creation also raises ethical concerns, including transparency, bias, and quality control, which must be carefully addressed to ensure responsible and impactful journalism in the digital age. Nonetheless, the potential of AI to augment human journalists' capabilities and revolutionize the news production process makes it an indispensable tool for the future of digital journalism. 5. CHALLENGES AND OPPORTUNITIES: The emergence of new trends in digital journalism presents both challenges and opportunities for journalists, news organizations, and society as a whole. This section explores the key challenges and opportunities associated with the evolving landscape of digital journalism. Ethical Concerns and Trustworthiness Challenge: Ensuring the integrity, accuracy, and fairness of news content in an era of fast-paced digital dissemination presents a significant challenge. AI-driven content creation raises concerns about transparency, bias, and accountability. Opportunity: Embracing ethical guidelines and best practices can enhance the trustworthiness and credibility of news organizations. Transparency about the use of AI technologies and editorial oversight can help mitigate concerns about bias and fairness. 1 The Fletcher School of Law and Diplomacy Professor Carolyn Gideon Assistant Professor of International Communication and Technology Policy, Director of the Hitachi Center for Technology and International Affairs Boston, January 6th 2016 Digital Journalism Business Models Do interactive features contribute to the success of digital journalism business models? Capstone Franziska Schwarzmann Candidate for the Master of Arts in Law and Diplomacy, class of 2015 Student ID: 1146514 2 Table of Contents Introduction and Research Question 4 Literature Review 6 Theoretical Approach 10 Data Description and Analysis 14 Conclusion 25 3 Introduction and Research Question Print news in Europe is no longer profitable. Advertising expenditures declined from 2009 to 2013 by 20%.1 In the same period, the total number of paid-for daily newspapers in these countries fell from 2130 to 1315. In only 4 years, 40 percent of the newspapers have either consolidated or quit the market. Yet, democracies need news. Even though not officially, journalism is commonly referred to as the fourth pillar of a democratic system, practicing control over the branches of government, informing citizens.2 Digital delivery and the instant availability of news online have disrupted the print news model- a business model that has worked for the newspaper industry for almost a century. As a result, traditional newspapers are struggling to come up with economically sustainable digital business models, while non-traditional market entrants, mostly from the digital technology industry, capture market share.3 How to make money with their digital offerings is the key question for newspapers. Although there is a body of literature regarding digital content, delivery and pricing, there is a gap in the research when it comes to determining the success factors of digital newspapers. Current research has been anchored in the thought patterns of the old business model. But what is needed now is unrestricted thinking into different potential revenue channels. In this paper I have set out to make a contribution to thought leadership on digital newspaper business models by asking what factors determine a successful digital newspaper. In particular, I am focusing on a certain feature that online news content offers: interactivity. Online news, compared to print news, provides opportunities for the reader. Online, the reader has the option4 to become active once the content has been consumed. Possible activities include to give feedback on content immediately, to speak to others about it or to easily share content via social 1 Calculations from the WAN-Ifra report, calculated by author. http://www.wptdatabase.org/. 2 Donica Mensing, “Online Revenue Business Model Has Changed Little since 1996,” Newspaper Research Journal 28, no. 2 (Spring 2007): 33, accessed November 8, 2014. 3 There is an active debate between traditional media players and digital companies that want to enter the market. Representatives of the former like David Pemsel, Guardian News & Medie chief executive, emphasize how long they’ve been around, thus implying that they know the business best: “When I have been on panels with BuzzFeed and others, they sort of try and say ‘the industry of which we are a part’. Some of those platforms have been around for three years and we’ve been around for 200. They try and step into our space.”(Robert Andrews, “Old Media’s Prospects Have Improved but the New Media Threat Looms Large,” The Guardian, accessed March 22, 2015, http://www.theguardian.com/media-network/2015/mar/20/new-media-threat-buzzfeed-vice.) On the other hand are massively venture backed, digitally obsessed start-ups like “briefme”, an app that ranks news according to their spread within social media. (https://i-lab.harvard.edu/venture-incubation/resident-teams/briefme). 4 Ester de Waal, Online News: Uses and Effects of News Websites, 2007, 245, http://dare.uva.nl/document/51514. 4 media or email. With time, there has been a social aspect added to reading the news and consumption patterns have changed in such a way that readers expect content to be interactive. This is why I have hypothesized that content interactivity has become a necessary condition for the success of newspapers. My hypothesis is that there is a positive statistically significant correlation between interactive website features and the success of journalism business models, represented by the amount of unique monthly users to a website. What will I be able to observe if my hypothesis holds true? The more interactive features on the website, the more unique monthly visitors a newspaper has. While I do acknowledge that there are multiple factors contributing the amount of unique monthly visitors such as content and advertising, I have tried controlling for some factors by limiting my sample to national daily and paid for newspapers from Europe. There will be a positive significant correlation between interactive website feature and unique monthly visitors. What will I be able to observe if my thesis does not hold true? There might be newspaper websites with high unique monthly visitors that do not have many interactive features on their websites. Second, there might be newspaper websites with a low number of unique monthly visitors and many interactive features. The hypothesis is neither proven nor disproven if there is a low number of unique monthly visitors and a low account of interactive website features. In order to answer the question, I self-designed and gathered a dataset with indicators measuring interactivity as well as standard indicators that I want to control for. I looked at the national daily paid for newspapers in Germany, Austria, Switzerland and the United Kingdom, summing up to a total of 24 newspapers. I reasoned these indicators to be necessary to indicate whether a website offering can be categorized as interactive, based on existing research and my reasoning. With the data set I have put together, it is possible to distinguish between the possible answers. That being said, I have included a section on learning that will be helpful for future research to gather more comprehensive data. I will first lay out the state of current literature on the topic of digital journalism business models. Next, I will explain my analytical framework and then turn to the structure of my data set and what questions it can answer. Finally, I include how my contribution to digital journalism research can be further used in the academic community as well as thought leadership in the industry. 5 Literature Review As key to further development of online news describes Richard van der Wurff the question of business models and revenue sources. 5 According to him, the internet offers a plethora of choices to publishers. His study “Print and Online Newspapers in Europe” gives an overview over what types of internet-specific features are offered by online newspapers and in what proportions. His definition and operationalization of interactivity are the basis for my data collection, because he distinguishes between interpersonal and content interactivity, whereby the former means that the reader can interact with other individuals and the latter implies that the reader can interact with the content. In addition to that, van der Wurff tests all newspaper websites for specific features on one day. An approach that I have used for my data collection as well. While van der Wurff’s research design and his operationalization serve in part as the basis for this paper’s operationalization and data gathering design, his book lacks a perspective: He only offers a theory in which online news is an addition to newspapers. His hypothesis is that the internet potentially functions as three different – complementary to print - channels, (1) the internet as a marketing channel, driving engagement with the customer (2) as an additional distribution channel to long- distance audiences or (3) a channel offering additional multimedia services for the core print audience. 6 Just as van der Wurff, Robert Picard has offered research that considers online as an extension of print. Robert Picard’s argument is the basis for my consideration that besides content there are other success factors of online journalism. He stresses that online has not triggered a revolution of content, but rather a revolution of content distribution and use. He argues that the success of online content is contingent upon obtaining and maintaining usage by offering products and services that understand the altered behavior of individuals and society. The basis for his thoughts is that there is a priori demand for a product; and new technologies offer a service extension to already existing customers: “Many believe the rhetoric that new and unique products and services are being created that will significantly alter the behavior of individuals and society. In reality, most of these products and services are just creating faster, easier, and more flexible means for consumers to do what they are already doing.” 7 The reader is given more choice and this 5 Richard van der Wurff and Edmund Lauf, Print and Online Newspapers in Europe. New methods of content presentation combined with technologies enabling more flexible use give an advantage to the consumer, for which he is willing to pay. However, the customer’s willingness to pay is a result of a perceived value added.8 Picard’s focus is on online content services. Thus, he does not give any examples of what this perceived value add could look like in the newspaper sphere. This is where van der Wurff’s research sets in: When van der Wurff published his study in 2004, he introduced a dichotomized way of thinking about the internet as a news medium: He said the internet could either be leveraged to improve the incumbent business model by improving secondary functions or the internet could be leveraged to transform a business model. Van der Wurff suggests that as soon as news is published online, there is an internet-specific feature added to a business model: Interactivity in content and interpersonally, multimediacy and immediacy. The definition of interactivity is highly contextual. Technologically, interactivity can be defined as “the extent to which users can participate in modifying the form and content of a mediated environment in real-time”. 9 There are also more journalism-specific definitions; they all have the common notion that digital journalism can empower the audience. These definitions differ regarding their sub-categorizations of interactivity: Communication researcher Jensen argues for four subconcepts of interactivity, Deuze categorizes three levels and Liu and Shrum focus on structural versus perceived interactivity. 10 In 2004, Bucy distinguished two types of interactivity: content interactivity, meaning consumers exercise control over editorial content and interpersonal interactivity, meaning real-time or delayed person-to-person conversations mediated by the news site.11 For the purposes of this paper, Bucy’s categorization makes the most sense as the simple dichotomized version of interactivity fits my research design the most. For years, interactivity has received praise as one of the key factors of digital journalism. In 2001, Deuze argued that interactivity is the added value of online journalism, J.D. Lasica claimed in 1998: “Online news publications have been so swept away by the graphic grandeur of the Web that they've all 8 Ibid. 9 Jens F. Jensen, “‘Interactivity’. Tracking a New Concept in Media and Communication Studies,” Nordicom Review 19, no. 1 (1998): 201. 10 Mark Deuze, “The Internet and Its Journalisms - Part I: A Typology of Online Journalism,” Online Journalism Review, July 11, 2002, 84, www.ojr.org/ojr/future/p1026407729.php; Deuze, “The Internet and Its Journalisms - Part I: A Typology of Online Journalism”; Yuping Liu and L.J. Shrum, “What Is Interactivity and Is It Always a Good Thing? Implications of Definition, Person, and Situation for the Influence of Interactivity on Advertising Effectiveness,” Journal of Advertising 31, no. 4 (2002): 55–64. 11 Erik Bucy, “Second Generation Net News: Interactivity and Information Accessibility in the Online Environment,” International Journal on Media Management 6, no. 1–2 (2004): 102–13. 7 but ignored the truly revolutionary promise of the Internet: interactivity.” 12 Content interactivity is the answer Vin Crosbie gives regarding the question of how to design a successful newspaper business model. In 2004, Vin Crosbie argued for content as well as channel customization, suggesting that readers needed to be able to decide what kind of news they received. In his opinion, the newspaper industry had wasted its energy on the wrong means and should have concentrated on creating more interactive papers tailored to readers’ individual interests. He stresses that declining readership numbers as well as declining willingness to pay may mostly be attributed to the lack of individual relevancy. The world’s first newspaper published stories that the editors anticipated would be of the greatest generic interest and this decision had been due to the technical limitations of printing, Crosbie continues. “Nearly four subsequent centuries of new printing technologies have increased the speed and image resolutions of analog presses, but that generic limitation has remained.”13 Reasoning that on average a reader is not interested in more than four articles of a generic newspaper, Crosbie concludes that this represents a “lousy satisfaction ratio”.14 Just as the invention of the transistor had initiated a revolution which enabled readers to choose a mix of media that best satisfied each of their own unique preferences, so should interactivity shatter that antiquated limitation. The industry, Crosbie continued, shall (1) use new technologies in order to match reader’s interests, (2) be more courageous to think out of channel silos and vision a truly converged product, (3) not limit itself to being a content producing machine, but instead deliver heroes of a content package. Even though his article in the Journalism Review is a solid analysis, it lacks any sort of academic justification because he presents his opinion, but does not prove that the generic limitations and reporters stuck in the culture thereof are to be blamed for the demise of newspapers. Interestingly, Ester de Waal produces an argument countering Crosbie’s suggestions. Her findings suggest that while generic print newspapers were good at making people read what they would otherwise not have read, online newspapers are less successful with this. 15 This sample of the research on interactivity suggests that it is not clear whether interactivity is a success factor for online news and thus far, this question has not been the subject of research. 12 Mark Deuze, “Online Journalism: Modelling the First Generation of News Media on the World Wide Web,” First Monday 6, no. 10, accessed November 28, 2015, http://firstmonday.org/ojs/index.php/fm/article/view/893; J.D. Lasica, “A Great Way to Strengthen Bonds. Online News Publications Should Take Better Advantage of the Net’s Interactivity.,” American Journalism Review, March 1998, http://ajrarchive.org/Article.asp?id=1789. 13 Vin Crosbie, “What Newspapers and Their Web Sites Must Do to Survive,” USC Annenberg Journalism Review, 2004, http://www.ojr.org/ojr/business/1078349998.php. 14 Ibid. 15 Ester de Waal, “Online and Print Newspapers: Their Impact on the Extent of the Perceived Public Agenda,” in Online News: Uses and Effects of News Websites, 2007, 69, http://dare.uva.nl/document/51514. 8 Customization of distribution channels is a factor for attracting younger target groups, according to the Media Insight Project. Their study about how millennials consume news found that “Millennials also appear to be drawn into news that they might otherwise have ignored because peers are recommending and contextualizing it for them on social networks, as well as on more private networks such as group texts and instant messaging.”16 88% of the respondents answered that they visited Facebook in order to get news. Why there is such a low pricing for online news has been the focus of a paper by Cunningham and Compaine. They found that online newspapers were offered for free because the early online subscription ideas failed and readers were not willing to pay. So the "free" model emerged, with the expectation - the hope - that advertisers would pay to reach the larger numbers that will click when the price is zero. Today, publishers are dealing with that legacy. Advertising revenues have grown, but not as fast as the revenue base for the printed newspaper has evaporated."17 One reason why online journalism has not been successful can be attributed to the fact that “newspaper managers as a whole do not appear to have altered their online strategies significantly”18, concludes Donica Mensing. The old business model for the print newspaper had two revenue sources, advertising and subscription. Newspapers have mainly tried to alter their ways around these revenue streams. Mensing attests that “despite massive changes in the market for news and aggressive competition for advertising revenue, newspaper managers as a whole do not appear to have altered their online strategies significantly during this nine year period.”19 The success of online newspapers has been looked at from different perspectives: In comparison to print editions, measured in its ability to generate revenue by attracting subscribers. However, what is not existent in the current literature is an analysis looking at what makes newspapers successful without directly linking success to monetization. 16 “How Millennials Get News,” American Press Institute, accessed March 22, 2015, http://www.americanpressinstitute.org/publications/reports/survey-research/millennials-news/. 17 Benjamin Compaine and Brendan Cunningham, “Addressing Real Issues for the Uncertain and Challenging Environment of the Media Industry,” Journal of Media Economics 23, no. 3 (August 31, 2010): 112. 18 Donica Mensing, “Online Revenue Business Model Has Changed Little since 1996,” Newspaper Research Journal 28, no. 2 (Spring 2007): 22, accessed November 8, 2014. 19 Ibid., 33. 9 Theoretical Approach My hypothesis is that there is a positive statistically significant correlation between interactive website features and the success of journalism business models, represented by the amount of unique monthly users to a website. Why? I base this hypothesis on two trends: The demand for print papers has been decreasing, and traffic to news sites depends on unique content as well as repeated visits. And “winning sites are those that are more convenient, user friendly, better organized and more visually appealing”.20 Interactivity has been described as the key element of economic viability for Web news organizations21, yet academically it has not been subject of research whether and if yes, what kind of interactivity is the secret sauce to news site traffic. Independent Variables I have operationalized interactivity with a subset of indicators which I will discuss in this section. This research is mainly based on van der Wurf’s framework of operationalization. The dimensions of interactivity are based on Bucy. Additionally, I have chosen 20 national daily, paid-for newspaper websites from four countries which I will introduce in this section. Content Interactivity Customization of Channels and of Content Donica Mensing notices two trends, but does not make a connection between them. First, from 1996 until 2005, advertising became more important as a source of revenue and concurrently more fragile due to online competition. 22Second, customization services can become a new source of revenue. This thesis follows the hypothesis that customization, hence the ability from a readers perspective to interact with content as well as the producer, reinforces advertising as a revenue channel, because once you understand your customer’s preference, you can target the advertising much more efficiently. This is especially in line with Mensing’s conclusion that “while advertising may generate sufficient revenue to keep some or many online newspapers operating, it appears they will need significantly to accelerate the scale and scope of this revenue source if they are to thrive successfully in a market that is fundamentally different from the market print newspapers have grown to dominate in 20 Paschalia-Lia Spyridou and Andreas Veglis, “Exploring Structural Interactivity in Online Newspapers: A Look at the Greek Landscape,” Journal Article (University of Illinois at Chicago University Library, April 28, 2008), http://firstmonday.org/article/view/2164/1960. 21 Ibid. 22 Donica Mensing, “Online Revenue Business Model Has Changed Little since 1996,” Newspaper Research Journal 28, no. 2 (Spring 2007): 22, accessed November 8, 2014. 10 many communities.”23 I see customization as a subset of interactivity, because it enables the reader to become active and choose that channel or content that she prefers.  Customization of Channels: Does the newspaper offer its content via different channels? I observe different channels (Email, Ebook, Smartphone, Tablet, Website) in order to understand what can be seen as standard and what might make a difference. This is a dichotomized variable, with the answer being either yes or no for each channel.  Customization of Content: Can the reader get individualized content packages? This is a dichotomized variable, with the answer being either yes or no.  Interactivity of content: Does the newspaper offer more content on the website in the form of a search engine, hyperlinks or an archive? I have included archives because Donica Mensing found in 2007 that “the revenue from archives, while very small, provided measurable income in 1996 and 2005.” From an interactive perspective, archives give the reader an opportunity to Hyperlinks are an option because Harvard’s Nieman Lab called them web journalism’s “currency of collaboration”24. Interpersonal Interactivity Social Media  Social Media: Indicates whether a newspaper has a Twitter channel. This is a dichotomized variable, with the answer being yes or no. I realize that there are several ways to operationalize Social Media and several social media channels like Facebook or Pinterest that matter. I have decided to go with Twitter as an indicator for journalism, because Twitter is a platform that has been knowingly used for the distribution and social dissemination of news.25 The presence of newspapers in social media channels offers several opportunities for interactivity. I have observed whether there is an opportunity to get directly in touch with the author, whether there is a commenting function underneath the article, whether the newspaper publishes letter to the editor on the website, whether the newspaper provisions a chatroom or a discussion forum and whether there is a sharing function for each article.  Digital Payment Method I included Digital Payment Methods because giving the reader the opportunity to pay as she wishes, and wherever, is a part of a customized and interactive offering. Whereas many millennials subscribe to paid services for movies, music and television and claim to pay for it themselves, they seem reluctant to pay for digital news. In-depth qualitative interviews revealed the sentiment valuing news as a universal right for which one should not have to pay. Without a doubt, a newspaper needs readers. Readers are a necessary condition for the success of a newspaper. However, a viable future business model might not depend on a consumer’s willingness to pay for news. Thus, readers might not be a sufficient condition for the monetary success of newspapers. Since the industry as a whole seems stuck and unable to answer the question of monetization, I wanted to choose a dependent variable on whose basis new research and ideas could be leveraged, thus my dependent variable is “unique monthly users”. The dependent variable, monthly unique visitors, comes from the World Association of Newspaper’s World Press Trends database. Scope In my selection of newspapers, I have chosen all national daily paid for newspapers in Germany, Austria, Switzerland and the United Kingdom. I limited this selection to those countries for which language does not produce a barrier to me. While all countries are European, Austria, Germany and Switzerland comprise one cultural region within Europe while the United Kingdom represents another language and a culture that stands in contrast to the German speaking region. Additionally, the UK, Germany and Austria are bound by policies of the European Union, Switzerland is not. SUMMARY:
Therefore, through a systematic literature review, this study aims to identify research trends and gaps in the field, conceptualize current paradigmatic views and therein provide clear propositions to guide future research in the entanglements of data journalism, collaboration, and business models. Through a systematic literature review, this study aims to identify research trends and gaps in the field, and conceptualize current paradigmatic views, thereby providing clear propositions to guide future research regarding the entanglements between data journalism, collaboration, and business models. To my knowledge, this study is original because of the comprehensiveness of the search conducted and the systematic review of studies creating the discourse around news organizations that use data and collaborative journalism as part of their business models, which to date have not been unified, though both practices have become popular with the digitization of news.

Abstract
--------

Despite it being somewhat of a niche project back in 2009, at this stage of its evolution, data journalism has gained significant traction to grow into a maturing field. However, little is known about the intersection of data journalism and collaboration in the news organizations’ business models from an infrastructure perspective, that is, key activities, key resources, and partner networks. These elements play an important role in the business model as they outline how an organization can optimize the efficiency within the business to provide the public with the best value proposition, in this case, data storytelling. Therefore, through a systematic literature review, this study aims to identify research trends and gaps in the field, conceptualize current paradigmatic views and therein provide clear propositions to guide future research in the entanglements of data journalism, collaboration, and business models. The originality of this study is rooted in the comprehensive search and systematic review of studies in the discourse around data and collaborative journalisms as part of the news organizations’ business models, which have not been unified to date, although both practices became popularized with the digitization of news organizations.

Keywords:

*   [Data journalism](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/Data+journalism)
*   [collaborative journalism](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/collaborative+journalism)
*   [business model](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/business+model)
*   [systematic literature review](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/systematic+literature+review)

Introduction
------------

In recent years, data journalism has attracted significant attention from scholars in different fields and produced an increasing number of publications in various areas (Mutsvairo [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)). Although data journalism was not widely popular in 2009, it has now become a well-established field with significant growth. This development can be attributed to the success of the Panama Papers investigation, a joint effort by over 370 journalists from 100 media outlets in 80 countries, which exposed the secretive operations of the unstructured data in the offshore economy (Lück and Schultz [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)). Journalists, designers, and technologists from different news outlets around the world worked collaboratively to bring this story to light in an engaging way. This project still serves as an important reminder of how collaborative journalism and data analytics can aid the news work process (Carson and Farhall [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)).

However, little is known about the intersection between data journalism and collaboration in news organizations’ business models from an infrastructure perspective, that is, key activities and key resources, and partner networks (Osterwalder and Pigneur [Citation 2010](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)). These elements play an important role in the business model because they outline how an organization can optimize efficiency within the business to provide the best value to the public: data-backed storytelling. Through a systematic literature review, this study aims to identify research trends and gaps in the field, and conceptualize current paradigmatic views, thereby providing clear propositions to guide future research regarding the entanglements between data journalism, collaboration, and business models.

Based on a systematic review of 55 peer-reviewed articles, taken from the Scopus® and Web of Science® databases, this study provides a descriptive analysis, with results that synthesize current research trends and account for gaps in the literature. To my knowledge, this study is original because of the comprehensiveness of the search conducted and the systematic review of studies creating the discourse around news organizations that use data and collaborative journalism as part of their business models, which to date have not been unified, though both practices have become popular with the digitization of news.

The aim of this systematic literature review, accordingly, is threefold. Using inclusion-exclusion criteria (Higgins et al. [Citation 2011](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#); Mohamed Shaffril, Samsuddin, and Abu Samah [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)), the first goal is to identify the general trends observed in the rapidly growing research on data journalism and collaboration. Second, this review looks at data journalism scholarship through the lens of business infrastructure, as proposed in the Business Model Canvas (BMC) by Osterwalder and Pigneur ([Citation 2010](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)). The BMC describes the rationale for how an organization creates, delivers, and captures value under nine building blocks that are somehow interconnected. These building blocks cover four main areas: customers, infrastructure, financial viability, and offering (Osterwalder and Pigneur [Citation 2010](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)). Infrastructure is the focus here due to its importance to the business models of news media organizations: it describes the key activities (understood as journalistic norms and routines), key resources (what is necessary to create value to the public), and partner network (found in collaborative journalism in the strategic alliances that news outlets adopt to deploy cooperative projects as well as other relationships that optimize operations and reduce business risks). Lastly, this study provides clear information about current scholarship on data journalism, collaboration, and business models, which consequently reveals research gaps and helps generate propositions for future research.

Method
------

Through a systematic literature review, this paper aims to locate and synthesize related research through organized, transparent, and replicable processes that include predefined search strings as well as standard inclusion and exclusion criteria (Higgins et al. [Citation 2011](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#); Mohamed Shaffril, Samsuddin, and Abu Samah [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)). This methodology is built on existing evidence, which allows researchers to identify gaps and directions for future research. In order to do so, qualitative techniques of pattern matching and explanation building have been employed to descriptively categorize published, peer-reviewed studies, highlighting commonalities and disparities using the eyeballing technique (Bhimani, Mention, and Barlatier [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)). Therefore, a descriptive, rather than a statistical, analysis of results is presented here.

Tranfield, Denyer, and Smart ([Citation 2003](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)) laid out a three-stage procedure for producing a systematic literature review: planning, execution, and guided reporting. The first step is to set the research objectives that support a broad scan of articles. This study focuses on peer-reviewed journal articles, as they are considered to have the greatest impact on research integrity and retention (Podsakoff et al. [Citation 2005](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)). To best capture the entanglements between data journalism, collaboration, and business models, this study uses predefined selection categorization driven by previous studies (Bhimani, Mention, and Barlatier [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#); Iden, Methlie, and Christensen [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#); Mohamed Shaffril, Samsuddin, and Abu Samah [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)).

In order to assess the range of paradigms, definitions, and operationalizations related to data journalism, as seen under the collaboration and business model lens, I adopted one of the four main areas of a business according to Osterwalder and Pigneur ([Citation 2010](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)) BMC. In it, business model patterns can be described in terms of the most important activities a company must perform (key activities), the most important assets required to perform these activities (key resources), and the partner network that makes the strategy work (key partnerships). This provides a framework for understanding how news media companies capture value by collaborating systematically with internal and external partners (Heft and Baack [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#); Westlund, Krumsvik, and Lewis [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#); Westlund and Ekström [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)). This reveals the conceptual and empirical evidence through a cross-disciplinary synthesis of data, which addresses the following research questions:

**RQ1.** What are the entanglements between data journalism, collaboration, and business models?

**RQ2.** How has current data journalism scholarship posed collaborative journalism in the news industry’s business models?

**RQ3.** From a collaborative perspective, what are the avenues of research for the future development of data journalism in the news industry?

 The second step was selecting the databases from which the initial list of articles would be retrieved. The Scopus® and Web of Science® databases were chosen for data collection, as they offer a broad range of indexed content from thousands of journals, and because of their relevance to the scientific literature. Scopus® is the largest “abstract and citation database of peer-reviewed literature” (Bhimani, Mention, and Barlatier [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#), 253), while Web of Science® is increasing in popularity among scholars. Prior studies have shown that there is significant overlap between the articles found on both databases. However, Scopus includes more exclusive journals (Mongeon and Paul-Hus [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/21670811.2023.2247449#)), so the initial list was conceived of data gathered from both platforms.
This study relied on a combination of keywords to search for relevant primary studies. In order to include only journal articles that covered “data journalism,” from the perspectives of “collaboration” and “business model,” terms such as “data-driven journalism” and “collaborative,” as well as similar terms, were used. Thus, the final search strings included:

*   “data journalism” AND “collaboration”;

*   “data journalism” AND “collaborative”;

*   “data-driven journalism” AND “collaboration”;

*   “data-driven journalism” AND “collaborative”;

*   “data journalism” AND “business model”;

*   “data-driven journalism” AND “business model.”

English-language, peer-reviewed articles were searched for these terms within their titles, abstracts, keywords, and body text. The collected database consists of 354 materials from Scopus® and 31 from Web of Science®. Collected data covers the period up until July 2021 (no time restrictions for the earliest publication). This means that the review includes all relevant publications were available up until that date. Any developments or new research published after July 2021 may not be included in the review, but they can be considered in future updates or revisions of this review.

Articles without the terms “collaboration,” “collaborative,” or “business model” in the title, abstract, and keywords were excluded. Duplicate articles were also excluded. Another exclusion criterion was articles not written in English (_n_ = 18). If an article was educational and not practical, it was also excluded from our final dataset, as this study seeks to understand the strategic value of data journalism from the practitioner’s viewpoint (_n_ = 42). The inclusion criteria were articles that included the above-mentioned terms or discussed the collaboration and business model aspects of data journalism. The final dataset consisted of 55 peer-reviewed articles, which covered a range of countries and regions, with the majority being from the United States and Europe (about 41%; Figure 2). In the case of Europe, it was considered articles that cover more than one European country. Similarly, Worldwide data journalism research refers to studies that encompass more than one country in different continents. Additionally, our dataset shows that most of these articles were published in recent years. In 2018, there was a significant increase to eight publications, followed by a slight decrease to seven in 2019. The most recent years, 2020 and 2021 (until June), saw the highest values with 16 and 11, respectively. This information is presented in Figure 3.

### Executive summary

News organisations are braced for multiple challenges in 2025 that will likely include further attacks from hostile politicians, continued economic headwinds, and battles to protect intellectual property in the face of rapacious AI-driven platforms. Changes to search, in particular, will become a major grievance for a news industry that has already lost social traffic and fears a further decline in visibility as AI interfaces start to generate ‘story like’ answers to news queries. The US election also highlighted the growing power of an alternative news ecosystem that includes partisan personalities and creators that often operate outside journalistic norms, and some say have now eclipsed the mainstream media in terms of both influence and trust.

Despite these difficulties many traditional news organisations remain optimistic about the year ahead – if not about journalism itself. Uncertain times tend to be good for business and the prospect of ‘Trump unleashed’[1](https://reutersinstitute.politics.ox.ac.uk/journalism-media-and-technology-trends-and-predictions-2025#footnote-035) could lead to a surge in web traffic and even in subscriptions. But this is by no means guaranteed. One key challenge will be to re-engage audiences that have fallen out of the habit of consuming news over recent years and to find ways of attracting the next generation. Many publishers will be looking to dramatically upscale the quality of their own websites, create more personalised news experiences, and invest further in audio and video. With consumer expectations moving at a rapid pace, embracing change while staying true to core journalistic values will be the key balancing trick for the year ahead.

**How media leaders view the year ahead**

These are the main findings from our industry survey, drawn from a strategic sample of 326 digital leaders from 51 countries and territories.

*   Just four in ten (41%) of our sample of editors, CEOs, and digital executives say they are confident about the prospects for journalism in the year ahead, with one in six (17%) expressing low confidence. Stated concerns relate to political polarisation, a rise in attacks on the press, and media capture – all of which in combination are seen as significant threats to journalism’s ability to operate freely.
*   More positively, just over half (56%) say they are confident about their own business prospects, a significant jump on last year’s figure. Many publishers expect traffic boosts amid the expected chaos of a second Trump presidency, others report continuing growth in online subscriptions, while others still think that the rapid growth of unreliable AI-generated content could bring audiences back to trusted media.
*   Meanwhile, around three-quarters (74%) of our survey respondents say they are worried about a potential decline in referral traffic from search engines this year. Data sourced for this report from analytics provider Chartbeat shows that aggregate traffic to hundreds of news sites from Google search remains stable for now but publishers fear the extension of AI-generated summaries to important news stories. This comes after big falls in referral traffic to news sites from Facebook (67%) and Twitter (50%) over the last two years.[2](https://reutersinstitute.politics.ox.ac.uk/journalism-media-and-technology-trends-and-predictions-2025#footnote-034)
*   In response to these trends, publishers will be putting more effort this year into building relationships with AI platforms (+56 net score[3](https://reutersinstitute.politics.ox.ac.uk/journalism-media-and-technology-trends-and-predictions-2025#footnote-033)) such as ChatGPT and Perplexity, both of which have been courting high-quality content in return for citations and/or money. With consumer attention switching to video, more publisher effort is also being planned for YouTube (+52) and TikTok (+48) – despite a possible ban in the United States early in 2025 – as well as Instagram (+43).
*   By contrast, publisher sentiment towards X/Twitter (-68 net score) has worsened this year following the politicisation of the network under Elon Musk. _The Guardian_, _Dagens Nyheter_, and _La Vanguardia_ are amongst those to have stopped posting on the platform, with Bluesky (+38) a key beneficiary. Google Discover (+27) is becoming a more important – if volatile – traffic source that has become critical to many news businesses over the last year. Our survey finds publishers ambivalent but also realistic about their dependence on platforms overall, with a similar proportion looking to cut ties (31%) as strengthen them (31%). Most of the rest (36%), are planning to maintain ties at existing levels.
*   On the business side, almost four in ten (36%) of our commercial publishers expect licensing income from tech and AI companies to be a significant revenue stream – twice as many as last year. But the amount and structure of any deals remain a point of contention. The majority of our survey respondents (72%) said they would prefer to see collective deals that benefited the whole industry rather than each company negotiating in their self-interest (19%), which is mostly what has been happening. A further 6% say they would rather not enter into any deals.
*   More widely, subscription and membership remain the biggest revenue focus (77%) for publishers, ahead of both display (69%) and native advertising (59%). The majority are now relying on three or four different revenue streams, including events (48%), affiliate revenue (29%), donations (19%), and related businesses (15%).
*   With subscription growth slowing, new product development is set to be a more important priority in the year ahead. More than a quarter of our publisher respondents say they are actively thinking about or planning to launch new products around games (29%) or education (26%) with one-fifth (20%) looking to launch an international or foreign language version. Many of these new products are likely to be bundled into ‘all-access’ subscriptions in a bid to reduce churn. At the same time more than four in ten (42%) say they’ll be looking to launch or trial a ‘youth’ product this year.
*   Meanwhile news organisations’ use of AI technologies continues to increase across all categories, with back-end automation (60%) considered very important by publisher respondents, many of whom have rolled out AI toolkits to support new workflows this year. The vast majority (87%) say that newsrooms are being fully or somewhat transformed by Gen AI, with just 13% saying not so much or not at all.
*   Audience-facing uses of AI are likely to proliferate in 2025 with publishers leaning into format personalisation as a way of increasing engagement. In our survey the majority said they would be actively exploring features that turn text articles into audio (75%), provide AI summaries at the top of stories (70%), or translate news articles into different languages (65%). Over half (56%) of respondents said they would be looking into AI chatbots and search interfaces. Not all these experiments will make it into full production but the direction of travel is clear.
*   More widely, respondents expect to see tech platforms developing and promoting their AI agents this year – many coming with improved conversational interfaces. OpenAI’s ChatGPT now comes with advanced voice features and both Siri and Alexa are getting an upgrade. Around one-fifth (20%) think these interfaces will be the ‘next big thing’, with half (51%) expecting more of a ‘slow burn’ impact.
*   Publishers are in two minds about whether the trends towards influencers and creators is good or bad for journalism. Around a quarter (27%) take a negative view, worrying that institutional news reporting could get squeezed out, but others (28%) are more positive, feeling that there is much that news organisations can learn in terms of storytelling creativity and building communities.
*   Linked to the above, some publishers worry about losing their editorial stars in a more personality-led ecosystem. But a bigger concern is around attracting and retaining talent in product and design (38%), data-science (52%), and engineering (55%) at a time when new product development is becoming more important than ever.

### 1. Journalism embattled but unbowed

While many publishers have confidence in their own news organisations, this year’s survey shows an alarming decline in confidence in journalism in just a few short years. Only four in ten (41%) say they are confident, down 19pp from the figure in our 2022 poll. The proportion that says they are not confident has risen from 10% to 17% over the same period.

It’s not difficult to see why this is happening. Across the world – and especially in societies that have become increasingly polarised – prominent politicians have been attacking or looking to undermine independent journalists, scrutinising their actions with threats, lawsuits, or worse. In a growing number of countries existing or new legislation, including national-security type laws, is being used to make it harder or more dangerous for journalists to operate.

There is a growing trend in Latin America to pass ‘foreign agent’ types of laws that mirror initiatives from Hungary or Russia. The goal is to crack down on civil society that exposes corruption, reports about organised crime and defends human rights or the environment
